title: Derivatives of Polynomials and Exponential Functions

epigraph.text:
By wisdom the Lord laid the earth's foundations, by 
understanding he set the heavens in place.

content.text:
Remember computing $\deriv{}{x}[x^{3}]$ from the limit definition in 
Chapter [ch:derivative]? It took half a page of expanding 
$(x+h)^{3}$, canceling terms, and factoring. The answer was $3x^{2}$, 
and it was correct—but the labor was immense.

Watch this:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[x^{3}\bigr] = 3x^{3-1} = 3x^{2}.
\]
One line. The same answer. Welcome to the **Power Rule**.

In this section, we build our first collection of differentiation rules. 
Each rule is *proved* from the limit definition of the derivative 
(the definition does the heavy lifting once, so we never have to repeat 
the work), and once established, it becomes a permanent tool. By the end 
of this section, you will be able to differentiate any polynomial—and 
the natural exponential—in seconds.
The Constant Rule
We start with the simplest possible function: a constant.

If $f(x) = c$ for some constant $c$, then the graph of $f$ is a horizontal 
line. Its slope is $0$ everywhere. Let us confirm this from the definition.

This should feel obvious: a function that never changes has a rate of 
change of zero. But notice how quickly the limit definition confirms it.
The Power Rule

The Power Rule is arguably the single most useful differentiation formula. 
Before we state it, let us look at the pattern.

In Chapter [ch:derivative], we computed the following derivatives from 
the limit definition:

The pattern is unmistakable: the exponent comes down as a coefficient, and 
the new exponent is one less than the original.

content.title: The Power Rule
content.content:
If $n$ is any positive integer, then
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr] = n\,x^{n-1}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We use the limit definition and the Binomial Theorem. Recall that
\[
 (x + h)^{n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k} x^{n-k}\,h^{k}
 = x^{n} + n\,x^{n-1}\,h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}\,h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}.
\]
Therefore,
$$
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{(x+h)^{n} - x^{n}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{x^{n} + n\,x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n} - x^{n}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{n\,x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\left[n\,x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h + \cdots + h^{n-1}\right] \\[6pt]
 &= n\,x^{n-1}.
$$
Every term after $n\,x^{n-1}$ contains a factor of $h$, so those terms all 
vanish in the limit.

content.title: Isaac Newton (1643--1727) and Gottfried Leibniz (1646--1716)
content.content:
Both Newton and Leibniz discovered the Power Rule independently in the late 
1600s. Newton, a devout Christian who wrote more about theology than 
mathematics, saw his scientific work as uncovering the rational order God 
had woven into creation. Leibniz, equally devout, described the universe 
as the "best of all possible worlds" designed by a perfect Creator. The 
Power Rule was among the earliest fruits of the calculus they both invented.

content.text:
The Power Rule as stated applies to positive integers. We will extend it 
later to all real exponents, but even now it handles an enormous class of 
functions.

content.title: Applying the Power Rule
content.solution:
By the Power Rule with $n = 7$,
\[
 f'(x) = 7x^{7-1} = 7x^{6}.
\]

content.title: Rewriting before differentiating
content.solution:
We first rewrite $g$ using a negative exponent: $g(x) = x^{-4}$. Applying 
the Power Rule (which we will prove works for negative integers shortly),
\[
 g'(x) = -4\,x^{-4-1} = -4\,x^{-5} = -\frac{4}{x^{5}}.
\]

content.text:
In fact, the Power Rule holds for *all* real exponents—integer, 
rational, and irrational. We will prove the rational case using implicit 
differentiation in Section [sec:implicit-diff] and the general case 
using logarithmic differentiation in Section [sec:log-invtrig-derivs]. 
For now, we state the general version.

content.title: The Power Rule (General Version)
content.content:
If $n$ is any real number, then
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr] = n\,x^{n-1}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Fractional and negative exponents
content.solution:
1. Write $f(x) = x^{1/2}$. Then $f'(x) = \frac{1}{2}\,x^{-1/2} 
 = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$.

2. $g'(x) = -3\,x^{-4} = -\dfrac{3}{x^{4}}$.

3. $h'(x) = \pi\,x^{\pi - 1}$. (Yes, $\pi$ is just a constant exponent.)

content.text: The Constant Multiple Rule

What happens if we multiply a function by a constant? Intuitively, 
multiplying by $c$ stretches the graph vertically by a factor of $c$, 
which should stretch the slope by the same factor.

content.title: The Constant Multiple Rule
content.content:
If $c$ is a constant and $f$ is differentiable, then
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[c\,f(x)\bigr] = c\,f'(x).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$$
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[c\,f(x)\bigr]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{c\,f(x+h) - c\,f(x)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\, c \cdot \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= c \cdot \limtozero{h}\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= c\,f'(x).
$$
We used the Constant Multiple Law for limits 
(Theorem [thm:limit-laws]) in the third step.

content.text: The Sum and Difference Rules

content.title: The Sum and Difference Rules
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are both differentiable, then
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr] = f'(x) + g'(x)
 \qquad\text{and}\qquad
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[f(x) - g(x)\bigr] = f'(x) - g'(x).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove the Sum Rule; the Difference Rule follows by writing $f - g = f + (-1)\cdot g$ 
and applying the Constant Multiple Rule.
$$
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{\bigl[f(x+h) + g(x+h)\bigr] - \bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr]}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\left[\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + \frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h}\right] \\[6pt]
 &= \limtozero{h}\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + \limtozero{h}\frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h} \\[6pt]
 &= f'(x) + g'(x).
$$
We used the Sum Law for limits in the third step.

content.text:
Together, the Constant Multiple, Sum, and Difference Rules tell us that 
differentiation is a **linear operation**: it respects addition and 
scalar multiplication. This means we can differentiate any polynomial 
*term by term*.

content.title: Differentiating a polynomial
content.solution:
We differentiate term by term:
$$
 p'(x) &= \deriv{}{x}\bigl[3x^{5}\bigr] - \deriv{}{x}\bigl[7x^{3}\bigr]
 + \deriv{}{x}\bigl[2x^{2}\bigr] - \deriv{}{x}\bigl[x\bigr]
 + \deriv{}{x}\bigl[4\bigr] \\[6pt]
 &= 3(5x^{4}) - 7(3x^{2}) + 2(2x) - 1 + 0 \\[6pt]
 &= 15x^{4} - 21x^{2} + 4x - 1.
$$

content.title: Rewriting radicals and fractions
content.solution:
First, expand (we will learn the Product Rule in Section [sec:product-quotient], 
but for now, we expand):
\[
 g(t) = t^{1/2} \cdot t^{2} + 3\,t^{1/2} = t^{5/2} + 3\,t^{1/2}.
\]
Now differentiate term by term:
\[
 g'(t) = \frac{5}{2}\,t^{3/2} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,t^{-1/2}
 = \frac{5}{2}\,t^{3/2} + \frac{3}{2\sqrt{t}}.
\]

content.title: Finding a tangent line
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = x^{4} - 6x^{2} + 1$ at $x = 2$.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Find the point.** At $x = 2$,
\[
 y = 2^{4} - 6(2)^{2} + 1 = 16 - 24 + 1 = -7.
\]
The point is $(2, -7)$.

**Step 2: Find the slope.** $y' = 4x^{3} - 12x$, so
\[
 y'(2) = 4(8) - 12(2) = 32 - 24 = 8.
\]

**Step 3: Write the equation.** Using point-slope form:
\[
 y - (-7) = 8(x - 2) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad y = 8x - 23.
\]

content.text: The Derivative of the Natural Exponential Function

We now turn to a function that is fundamentally different from any polynomial: 
the natural exponential function $f(x) = e^{x}$.

In a polynomial like $x^{n}$, the variable is the *base* and the exponent 
is a constant. In $e^{x}$, the base is a constant and the *exponent* is 
the variable. This difference is profound.

Let us apply the limit definition:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[e^{x}\bigr] 
 = \limtozero{h}\frac{e^{x+h} - e^{x}}{h}
 = \limtozero{h}\frac{e^{x}\cdot e^{h} - e^{x}}{h}
 = e^{x}\cdot\limtozero{h}\frac{e^{h} - 1}{h}.
\]
Everything depends on the value of $\limtozero{h}\dfrac{e^{h} - 1}{h}$.
We can estimate this limit numerically:

The limit is $1$. In fact, this is one of the defining properties of the 
number $e$: it is the unique base for which $\limtozero{h}\frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} = 1$.

This is one of the most remarkable results in all of calculus: *the 
natural exponential function is its own derivative*. No other function 
(aside from constant multiples of $e^{x}$ and the zero function) has this 
property. The rate at which $e^{x}$ grows is *exactly equal* to its 
current value.

content.title: The tangent line to the exponential
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = e^{x}$ at $x = 0$.
content.solution:
At $x = 0$: $y = e^{0} = 1$, and $y' = e^{0} = 1$. The tangent line is
\[
 y - 1 = 1(x - 0) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad y = x + 1.
\]
This is the linearization of $e^{x}$ near $x = 0$: for small $x$, 
$e^{x} \approx 1 + x$.

content.title: Combining rules
content.solution:
Since $\frac{1}{5}$ is a constant factor,
\[
 h'(x) = \frac{1}{5}\bigl(2e^{x} + 3x^{2}\bigr) = \frac{2e^{x} + 3x^{2}}{5}.
\]

content.title: Finding horizontal tangent lines
content.problem:
Find all points on the curve $y = x^{3} - 12x + 1$ where the tangent line 
is horizontal.
content.solution:
A tangent line is horizontal when $y' = 0$. We have
\[
 y' = 3x^{2} - 12 = 3(x^{2} - 4) = 3(x-2)(x+2).
\]
Setting $y' = 0$ gives $x = 2$ or $x = -2$.

At $x = 2$: $y = 8 - 24 + 1 = -15$. At $x = -2$: $y = -8 + 24 + 1 = 17$.

The tangent line is horizontal at $(2, -15)$ and $(-2, 17)$.

content.title: The Power Rule vs.\ the Exponential Rule
content.content:
The Power Rule applies when the *base* is the variable: 
$\deriv{}{x}[x^{n}] = nx^{n-1}$.

The Exponential Rule applies when the *exponent* is the variable: 
$\deriv{}{x}[e^{x}] = e^{x}$.

Do **not** confuse them! For instance, $\deriv{}{x}[x^{5}] = 5x^{4}$ 
(Power Rule), but $\deriv{}{x}[5^{x}]$ requires techniques we will develop 
in Section [sec:log-invtrig-derivs].

content.text: Summary of Rules So Far

marginNotes.text:
Do you see someone skilled in their work? 
They will serve before kings; they will not serve before officials of 
low rank.

Great are the works of the Lord, studied by 
all who delight in them.

God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful and 
increase in number and fill the water in the seas, and let the birds increase 
on the earth."

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on 
the shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
For since the fabric of the universe is most 
perfect and the work of a most wise Creator, nothing at all takes place in 
the universe in which some rule of maximum or minimum does not appear.
